title: spawnOne() silently swallows factory exceptions, hiding pool starvation cause
description: In the `acquire()` method's spawn-under-cap loop, a bare `catch { break; }` at line 508 swallows any exception thrown by `this.factory()` or `spawnOne()`. The caller receives fewer handles than requested with no diagnostic about why the factory failed (OOM, Worker construction error, etc.). The empty catch makes it impossible to distinguish 'pool at cap' from 'factory threw', and the pool may silently degrade to serial mode without logging.

title: Worker addEventListener wiring failures silently swallowed, hiding lifecycle errors
description: In `spawnOne()`, `worker.addEventListener` calls are wrapped in try/catch with a `wiringOk = false` fallback 'for test doubles'. If real Worker construction succeeds but addEventListener throws (e.g. a subclassed Worker with a bug), the pool marks `wiringOk = false` and calls `worker.terminate()` but the thrown error is completely lost. The only observable effect is `throw new Error('lifecycle wiring failed')`, which loses the original cause.

title: ensureLoaded() silently swallows all postMessage errors per worker
description: In `ensureLoaded()`, the entire postMessage dispatch for a worker is wrapped in a bare `catch {}` (line ~758). A postMessage failure (detached SAB, worker already terminated, structured-clone error on large bytes) is silently ignored. The worker will later receive a decode request without having the bytes loaded, and it will reply with UNKNOWN_BYTES_ID. The actual cause (postMessage throw) is never surfaced.

title: Watchdog timeout uses incorrect 1.5x multiplier when requestTimeoutMs is zero or undefined
description: When `requestTimeoutMs` is undefined (the common case where no requestTimeout is set on the pool), the watchdog timeout is computed as `Math.max(10_000, (requestTimeoutMs ?? 0) * 1.5)` = `Math.max(10_000, 0)` = 10 seconds. This is correct by coincidence, but the intent is unclear. More critically, when `requestTimeoutMs` is 0 (explicitly disabled), the watchdog still fires at 10 seconds and kills the worker, which may be intentional but is not documented. The guard `if (!job.requestTimer)` on line 155 is correct, but the fallback arithmetic reads as if the watchdog is derived from requestTimeout when it is not.

title: destroy() setInterval leak when active workers never drain: interval not cleared on grace timeout
description: In `destroy()`, a `setInterval` polling loop is created to wait for `this.active.size === 0`. If the active workers never drain within `graceMs`, `Promise.race` resolves via the grace timeout, but the `setInterval` `iv` is never `clearInterval`'d because the interval's own `r()` callback was never called. The interval continues firing every 10 ms for the lifetime of the environment until `active.size` eventually hits 0 (which may never happen if workers were force-terminated). This is a timer leak.

title: getOrParseJxtcTileIndex: tilesX * tilesY multiplication can overflow before bounds check
description: In `getOrParseJxtcTileIndex`, `numTiles = header.tilesX * header.tilesY` is computed in plain JS number arithmetic. `header.tilesX` and `header.tilesY` each come directly from 32-bit little-endian uint32 fields in the JXTC container. If both are near `2^16`, their product overflows JS integer precision (exceeds 2^53? No, but `tilesX=65535, tilesY=65535` = ~4.3e9 which exceeds 2^32 and is an exact double). The real issue is the size check `bytes.byteLength < 32 + numTiles * 8` can produce a correct guard in IEEE double but `new Uint32Array(numTiles)` with ~4e9 elements would throw RangeError. parseJxtcHeader validates the decoded pixel cap but not the tile count.

title: ensureIccProfile silently swallows all errors and always resolves null on failure
description: The inner async IIFE in `ensureIccProfile` has a blanket `catch { return null; }` that discards all decode errors. An OOM, WASM crash, or JXL parse error on a valid file will silently produce `iccProfile = null` with no diagnostic. Since this result is then cached on the source object, subsequent calls also return null. Callers that depend on ICC for correct colour reproduction will silently operate in wrong colourspace without any error signal.

title: decodeTiledViewportPooled acquires handles but never waits for ready before fallback decision
description: At line 1257, `await Promise.all(liveHandles.map((h) => h.ready))` waits for all handles to be ready, but throws if any handle's `ready` promise rejects (a ready-failure for a spawned worker). The `ready` promise in `spawnOne` is resolved but never rejected; however if the worker fires 'error' before 'ready', `destroyHandle` is called which resolves the ready promise early. The issue is there is no `.catch` guard here — if the pool is partially constructed and some `ready` promises reject, the entire viewport decode fails without attempting the direct fallback path that follows at line 1259.

title: Progressive Phase 1 DC errors with 'fail-fast' policy propagate as non-PyramidError, losing typed error code
description: In the dc-then-final Phase 1 loop, when `errorPolicy !== 'skip-tile'`, the caught `e` from `decodeTileBytesProgressive` is re-thrown bare (`throw e`). If the error is a `PyramidError('JXTC_PARSE', ...)` it preserves its type, but if `createDecoder` throws a plain `Error` or a DOMException, callers cannot distinguish tile parse failure from decode infrastructure failure (OOM, Worker crash). The pooled path wraps errors into PyramidError uniformly; the direct path does not.

title: prewarmAsync fire-and-forget in constructor creates unhandled promise rejection on spawn failure
description: In the `PyramidWorkerPool` constructor, `void this.prewarmAsync(this.minIdle)` discards the returned promise. If `prewarmAsync` throws (e.g. `this.factory()` throws synchronously in `spawnOne`), the rejection is unhandled and will surface as an uncaught promise rejection in the host environment. The `prewarmAsync` body has no top-level try/catch, only the `await Promise.all(spawned.map(h => h.ready.catch(() => {})))` guard for ready-failures after spawn succeeds.

title: Viewport cache completeness check silently treats skipTiles.size > 0 as incomplete, but may cache partial result anyway via cacheStore
description: The `complete` guard at line 416 correctly excludes `skipTiles && skipTiles.size > 0` from viewport caching. However, `cacheStore` is only called when `complete` is true, so skipped-tile viewports are not cached under the viewport key. The issue is that `failedTiles` only includes tiles that errored, not skipped tiles. A caller who resumes decode by passing `skipTiles` for previously-decoded tiles then calls without `skipTiles`, expecting the cache to be warm, but the cache is empty because the prior partial decode was not cached. This is a correctness gap, not a crash, but may confuse caller expectations.

title: decodeTilesParallel: error from one coroutine aborts remaining but does not remove outer abort listener before throwing
description: In `decodeTilesParallel`, if a tile decode fails, `controller.abort()` is called and `firstErr` is set. The `await Promise.all(coros)` line collects all coroutine results. When `failed` is true, the outer abort signal listener (`outerAbortListener`) is removed in the `finally` block. However, if `Promise.all` itself throws (which it does not since coros never reject — they set `failed=true` and break), the removal is still protected by `finally`. The actual risk: if `opts.signal` is aborted externally between the `failed=true` moment and the `finally` block, `outerAbortListener` fires and calls `controller.abort()` again on an already-aborted controller — this is harmless but the duplicate abort signal listener is not removed until `finally`, creating a window where `outerAbortListener` can fire twice.

title: Aspect ratio validation uses division without guarding height=0
description: In `parsePyramidManifest`, the aspect check at line 160 computes `width / height` inline. If `height` is 0 (which `requireNumber` allows since it only checks for finite-ness), the division produces `Infinity`. The subsequent check `Math.abs(aspect - Infinity) > 1e-3` is always true, so a manifest with `height=0` and any `aspect` will throw a validation error with a confusing message about aspect ratio inconsistency rather than about zero height. The real guard (positive integer dimensions) belongs at the level validator, not only at `createLevelSource`.

title: release() destroys handles with pending jobs but does not reject their promises before destroy
description: In `release()`, when a handle has `h.pending.size > 0` it calls `destroyHandle(h, 'release of dead/inflight')`. `destroyHandle` does call `cleanupPendingJob` and `job.reject(POOL_DESTROYED)` for each pending job. However, if the caller of `release()` holds a reference to those pending decode promises, those promises are rejected from within `destroyHandle` which is fine. The subtle risk: if `release()` is called by `decodeTiledViewportPooled`'s `finally` block while tile coroutines still have pending jobs (because the abort path in `decodeTilesParallel` manually deletes from `h.pending` before calling `job.reject`), a race exists where `destroyHandle` in `release` tries to reject an already-settled promise. The `job.reject` has no settled guard unlike `doReject`.

category: no structured error taxonomy / sentinel errors
title: PyramidErrorCode uses open `string & {}` union, preventing exhaustive handling by callers
description: The `PyramidErrorCode` type includes `(string & {})` as a fallback. While this allows forward-compatible codes, it means switch/exhaustive checks on error codes are impossible at the TypeScript level. Callers who want to handle ABORTED vs TIMEOUT vs JXTC_PARSE distinctly must use string comparison without compile-time verification. A closed union (or a separate `UnknownPyramidErrorCode = string` type for truly unknown codes) would enable type-safe exhaustive handling and surface missing cases at compile time.

title: acquire() maxWaitMs defaults to 60ms with no diagnostic when timeout fires
description: The `acquire()` method uses a 60ms default maxWaitMs via `opts?.maxWaitMs ?? 60`. When the timeout fires, the waiter is silently removed and `resolve(got)` is called with whatever partial handles were obtained. There is no logging, no thrown error, and no indication to the caller that they received fewer handles than requested due to timeout vs capacity. Adding a structured timeout result (or a small log) would help diagnose pool sizing issues in production.

title: decodeTileBytesProgressive: events() async iterator abandonment on decoder.push() throw leaks decoder
description: In `decodeTileBytesProgressive`, the async IIFE `drainOutcome` starts iterating `decoder.events()` concurrently with `await decoder.push(tileBytes)`. If `decoder.push()` throws synchronously, the `finally { await Promise.resolve(decoder.dispose()).catch(() => {}); }` block runs, disposing the decoder. However, `drainOutcome` is still running concurrently — it is iterating an async iterator whose underlying generator may hold references to the (now disposed) decoder. The `await drainOutcome` after push/close means the race is resolved before function exit, but if `decoder.push()` throws and the function exits via the `finally` block, `drainOutcome` is abandoned (no await on it). The abandoned iterator will either complete harmlessly or throw an unhandled rejection.

title: getOrCreatePool: pool.destroy(0) called fire-and-forget when factory changes, hiding destroy errors
description: When a factory conflict is detected with no active decodes, `void pool.destroy(0)` is called fire-and-forget. If `destroy` throws or rejects (e.g. during visibility listener cleanup), the error is silently discarded. More critically, `pool = null` and `poolFactory = null` are set synchronously before destroy completes. If the partially-destroyed pool has inflight timers that fire after reassignment, those timers reference the old pool state that is now orphaned.
